name: Arabica coffee (food medium)
basis: per 100 g green Arabica coffee bean (Coffea arabica), dry weight; roasting effects noted per compound

components.name: sucrose
components.source: Ky et al. 2001 Food Chem doi:10.1016/S0308-8146(01)00204-7; PMC8632381 (Indonesian Arabica, 31-56 mM in-bean)
components.evidence: Dominant low-MW soluble carbon store of green Arabica beans; reported range 5.8-15.7 g/100g across origins, midpoint ~8. Largely degraded (to <2 g/100g) during roasting via caramelisation/Maillard.

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: Ky et al. 2001 Food Chem doi:10.1016/S0308-8146(01)00204-7 (reducing sugars)
components.evidence: Free reducing sugars are minor in green beans (~0.1-0.3 g/100g each glucose/fructose); consumed in Maillard reactions on roasting.

components.name: D-fructose
components.source: Ky et al. 2001 Food Chem doi:10.1016/S0308-8146(01)00204-7 (reducing sugars)
components.evidence: Minor free reducing sugar in green beans, ~0.1-0.3 g/100g; declines with roasting.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Acids-in-coffee meta-analysis, Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767 (12.1 +/- 1.6 g/kg green Arabica)
components.evidence: Predominant tricarboxylic organic acid in green Arabica; decreases markedly with roast level. Key utilisable carbon/energy acid usually missing from FooDB dumps.

components.name: malic acid
components.source: Acids-in-coffee meta-analysis doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767 (4.4 +/- 0.8 g/kg green Arabica)
components.evidence: Second major C4 organic acid; falls to <0.05 g/100g in medium roast. Absent from the FooDB record - added.

components.name: quinic acid
components.source: Acids-in-coffee meta-analysis doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767 (6 +/- 0.8 g/kg green Arabica)
components.evidence: Free quinic acid; rises further on roasting as chlorogenic acids hydrolyse. Utilisable carbon source.

components.name: acetic acid
components.source: Acids-in-coffee meta-analysis doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767 (4 +/- 3 g/kg green Arabica); PMC8632381 (1.5-3.4 mM)
components.evidence: Present in green beans and increases proportionally with roast level (dark roasts highest).

components.name: lactic acid
components.evidence: Minor, roughly constant across roast; slightly higher in roasted than green. Amount approximate.

components.name: formic acid
components.evidence: Trace/minor volatile acid, increases with roasting. Amount approximate.

components.name: chlorogenic acids (total, mainly 5-caffeoylquinic acid)
components.evidence: Largest phenolic pool of green coffee, 5-CQA dominant; partially hydrolysed to caffeic + quinic acid on roasting. BiGG lacks the intact esters (isomers kept in keep_uncovered) but the pool is a defining, quantitatively major coffee metabolite.

components.name: caffeine
components.evidence: Defining purine alkaloid of coffee (Arabica ~1.2%, lower than Robusta); largely a xenobiotic/growth-inhibitory N compound rather than a favourable substrate. No BiGG metabolite -> kept uncovered but quantified.

components.name: trigonelline
components.evidence: N-methylnicotinate; ~1 g/100g green, thermally demethylated to nicotinic acid (niacin) on roasting. No BiGG metabolite -> kept uncovered but quantified.

components.name: protein (total)
components.source: Hindawi Int J Agronomy 2022 art.1317341; PMC7466179
components.evidence: Green Arabica storage protein 10-13% dw. Major bound/free amino acids (glutamate, aspartate, alanine, asparagine, then arginine, proline, lysine) - the individual amino acids already in the FooDB record are retained as protein-derived N sources.

components.name: L-alanine (free)
components.evidence: Highest free amino acid in green Arabica (~800 ug/g); rest of free-AA pool (asparagine, glutamate, aspartate, GABA) smaller. Free amino acids are the readily assimilable N/C fraction.

components.name: L-asparagine (free)
components.evidence: Second most abundant free amino acid in green Arabica; acrylamide precursor on roasting.

components.name: GABA (4-aminobutanoate, free)
components.evidence: Non-protein free amino acid, rises with dry post-harvest processing.

components.name: total lipid (coffee oil)
components.source: Hindawi Int J Agronomy 2022 art.1317341; PMC8121985
components.evidence: Green Arabica oil 15-17% dw (higher than Robusta), predominantly triacylglycerols plus diterpene esters (cafestol/kahweol) and tocopherols; fatty-acid breakdown given below.

components.name: linoleic acid (C18:2, n-6)
components.evidence: Most abundant fatty acid of coffee oil (~40%); 0.40 x 15 g oil.

components.name: palmitic acid (C16:0)
components.evidence: Second most abundant fatty acid (~37%); 0.37 x 15 g oil.

components.name: oleic acid (C18:1, n-9)
components.evidence: ~8.5-9.9% of coffee oil; 0.09 x 15 g oil.

components.name: stearic acid (C18:0)
components.evidence: ~7.6% of coffee oil; ~0.077 x 15 g oil.

components.name: arachidic acid (C20:0)
components.evidence: ~2.1% of coffee oil; 0.021 x 15 g oil.

components.name: alpha-linolenic acid (C18:3, n-3)
components.evidence: ~1.5% of coffee oil; already present in FooDB record, now quantified.

components.name: potassium
components.evidence: By far the dominant mineral of coffee (~2% dw); principal cation.

components.name: magnesium
components.source: Springer EFRT 2025 doi:10.1007/s00217-025-04664-7 (1758 ug/g); Arabica > Robusta
components.evidence: Second most abundant mineral.

components.name: phosphorus
components.evidence: Present as phytate/phosphate; value approximate (not in the specific ICP source fetched), medium confidence.

components.name: calcium
components.evidence: Confirmed FooDB entry, now quantified.

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Single consolidated iron value; the FooDB record's 'Iron', 'Iron (Fe3+)' and 'Fe2+ mitochondria' are the same element (compartment/oxidation-state artifacts) - see remove[].

components.name: manganese
components.evidence: Coffee is comparatively Mn-rich (~2-4 mg/100g); value approximate, medium confidence.

components.name: zinc
components.evidence: Confirmed trace nutrient, quantified.

components.name: copper
components.evidence: Trace essential micronutrient ~1-2 mg/100g; value approximate.

components.name: sodium
components.evidence: Naturally very low in coffee beans; retained as minor mineral.

components.name: sulfate/sulfur
components.evidence: Retained as an essential S source (from sulfur amino acids/inorganic S); not separately quantified here.

components.name: niacin (nicotinic acid)
components.source: USDA / roasted-coffee data (16.8 mg/100g roasted); Ky 2001 (trigonelline precursor)
components.evidence: Low as free niacin in GREEN beans (~0.5-2 mg/100g); rises steeply on roasting (roasted ground coffee ~10-20 mg/100g) via thermal demethylation of trigonelline. This is coffee's defining vitamin.

components.name: alpha-tocopherol (vitamin E)
components.evidence: Coffee oil carries alpha- and gamma-tocopherol; combined a few mg/100g. Confirmed FooDB entries, retained.

components.name: gamma-tocopherol
components.evidence: Predominant tocopherol homologue in coffee oil; approximate.

components.name: thiamine (B1)
components.evidence: Trace; retained as micronutrient. Approximate.

components.name: riboflavin (B2)
components.evidence: Trace; retained. Approximate.

components.name: choline
components.evidence: Genuine minor component of coffee; retained, unquantified.

components.name: folate (B9)
components.evidence: Low but plausible in a plant seed; retained as unquantified micronutrient.

components.name: pantothenic acid (B5)
components.evidence: Plausible plant-seed vitamin; retained, unquantified.

components.name: pyridoxine (B6)
components.evidence: Plausible plant-seed vitamin; retained, unquantified.

remove.name: Cholesterol
remove.reason: Animal sterol, biologically implausible in a plant seed; FooDB predicted, not a coffee growth nutrient. Coffee's sterol fraction is phytosterols/diterpenes (cafestol, kahweol).

remove.name: 17alpha-ethynylestradiol
remove.reason: Synthetic pharmaceutical estrogen; absurd as a natural coffee constituent - FooDB predicted/contaminant artifact.

remove.name: Cyanocobalamin
remove.reason: Vitamin B12 is not synthesized by plants/coffee; predicted, not measured. Remove.

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: Preformed vitamin A occurs only in animal foods; implausible in green coffee. FooDB predicted.

remove.name: Beta-Carotene
remove.reason: Green coffee beans are not a carotenoid source; entry is predicted/negligible, not a growth nutrient.

remove.name: L-Ascorbate
remove.reason: Vitamin C is minimal in green coffee and destroyed on roasting; predicted rather than a real growth substrate.

remove.name: Phytomenadione
remove.reason: Vitamin K1 negligible in coffee beans; FooDB predicted, not a growth-relevant nutrient.

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace cobalt; FooDB elemental dump, not a defined coffee nutrient.

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Trace/contaminant element, not a growth nutrient of coffee.

remove.name: Selenium
remove.reason: Trace, highly soil-variable, not a defining coffee nutrient; drop as noise.

remove.name: Molybdate
remove.reason: Trace micronutrient from FooDB elemental dump; not quantitatively relevant for coffee medium.

remove.name: Iodine c
remove.reason: Not a coffee nutrient; trace/contaminant FooDB entry.

remove.name: Fluoride
remove.reason: Trace, not a growth nutrient; remove.

remove.name: Fe2+ mitochondria
remove.reason: Compartment/redox-state artifact duplicating 'Iron'; consolidated into a single iron entry.

remove.name: Iron (Fe3+)
remove.reason: Oxidation-state duplicate of 'Iron'; consolidated into a single iron entry.

remove.name: Starch C12H20O10
remove.reason: Green coffee stores little starch; its reserve carbohydrates are cell-wall polysaccharides (galactomannan, arabinogalactan). Retain only trace; starch entry is misleading as a major substrate.

remove.name: Dextrin C12H20O10
remove.reason: Not a genuine green-coffee constituent; dextrins are process/roast artifacts. Predicted noise.

missing_added: sucrose with amount (dominant soluble carbon of green beans)
citric acid, malic acid, quinic acid, acetic acid, lactic acid, formic acid (organic-acid pool - the main FooDB omission)
total protein and major free amino acids (alanine, asparagine, GABA)
total lipid + full fatty-acid breakdown (linoleic, palmitic, oleic, stearic, arachidic)
quantitative minerals (K, Mg, P, Ca, Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, Na)
niacin with green-vs-roasted note; tocopherols quantified
keep_uncovered: neochlorogenic acid, cryptochlorogenic acid (3- and 4-CQA isomers)
caffeic acid, p-coumaric acid, ferulic acid, cinnamic acid (hydroxycinnamates)
catechols and volatile phenols (guaiacol/2-methoxyphenol, 4-vinylguaiacol, 4-ethylguaiacol, pyrogallol) - genuine roast/phenolic volatiles
sources: Ky et al. 2001, Food Chemistry 75:223 doi:10.1016/S0308-8146(01)00204-7 (caffeine, trigonelline, chlorogenic acids, sucrose in Arabica)
Acids in coffee: review and meta-analysis, Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr 2022 doi:10.1080/10408398.2021.1957767 (organic acids green/roasted)
Metabolite Profiles of Green Beans of Indonesian Arabica Varieties, PMC8632381 (sugars, acids, alkaloids, amino acids in mM)
Green-coffee proximate composition, Int J Agronomy 2022 art.1317341 (protein, lipid, carbohydrate, ash)
Green Arabica coffee oil fatty-acid composition, PMC8121985; Agronomy 2023 doi:10.3390/agronomy13102560
Green-coffee mineral ICP-OES, Eur Food Res Technol 2025 doi:10.1007/s00217-025-04664-7; PMC6778417 (K, Mg, Ca, Fe, Zn, Cu, Na)
Free amino acids in green coffee, Eur Food Res Technol doi:10.1007/BF01231078
USDA FoodData Central / roasted-coffee panel (niacin ~16.8 mg/100g roasted, tocopherols)
notes: Curated as green Arabica coffee beans (dry weight); roast-dependent changes flagged per compound (sucrose and chlorogenic acids fall, quinic/acetic/niacin rise). Dominant utilisable carbon/energy substrates for a growth medium are sucrose (~8 g/100g) plus the organic-acid pool (citric > quinic > malic > acetic, ~2.6 g/100g total) and the chlorogenic/quinic acid pool; caffeine (~1.2 g/100g) and trigonelline (~1 g/100g) are largely inhibitory/xenobiotic N compounds rather than favourable substrates. Potassium is the overwhelmingly dominant mineral. Main FooDB corrections: added the entire organic-acid pool and specific sugars, and removed animal-sterol/pharmaceutical/predicted-vitamin noise (cholesterol, 17a-ethynylestradiol, B12, retinol, vitamin K1) plus trace-element and duplicate-iron artifacts.
